skill.name: runcomfy-cli
skill.description: Use the RunComfy CLI to authenticate, discover model schemas, run media generation and editing models, poll jobs, and download outputs.
skill.summary: Run RunComfy media models from the command line with authentication, polling, and output downloads.
skill.author: runcomfy-com

security_audit.summary: The static external command and URL findings are mostly expected documentation for a RunComfy CLI skill, not evidence of malware. Confirmed risks are the authenticated network workflow, local token handling, environment token use, and generated output downloads. No prompt injection attempt or confirmed malicious intent was found.

medium_findings.title: Networked CLI Sends User Inputs to RunComfy
medium_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE. The skill instructs agents to run RunComfy commands that submit prompts and assets to model-api.runcomfy.net, poll jobs, and download results. This is expected functionality but requires user awareness because inputs and media URLs leave the local environment.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The documented workflow explicitly describes POST, GET, DELETE, and download behavior against RunComfy domains. The endpoints are disclosed and aligned with the skill purpose, so this is a legitimate but real data-flow risk.

medium_findings.title: Credential Storage and Environment Token Use
medium_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE. The skill documents token storage in ~/.config/runcomfy/token.json and RUNCOMFY_TOKEN for CI. It includes safeguards against logging or committing tokens, but the skill still handles credentials.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The credential file path and environment variable are named directly in the skill. The same section contains protective guidance, reducing concern about malicious intent but not removing credential-handling risk.

medium_findings.title: Shell Scripting Examples Need Trusted Inputs
medium_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE as operational risk, not malicious code. Batch and polling examples use command substitution, jq, date, and retry logic. Users should avoid inserting untrusted shell fragments into these examples.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples are ordinary shell automation and one example uses jq to JSON-encode prompt text. Risk depends on user modification, so confidence is high for operational concern but not for exploitation.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded RunComfy URLs Are Expected
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE for exfiltration. The URLs point to RunComfy homepage, documentation, catalog pages, API endpoints, and output download domains disclosed by the skill. No unrelated callback endpoint was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network destinations are first-party RunComfy domains and match the described CLI service. I did not find evidence of hidden or unrelated third-party exfiltration endpoints.

low_findings.title: Filesystem Findings Are Documented Storage and Downloads
low_findings.description: PARTIAL TRUE POSITIVE. The hidden home path is documented credential storage and output-dir downloads write files. The path traversal flag on the example URL is only an ellipsis placeholder.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file paths are explicitly described and mostly expected for login and downloads. The line 114 traversal signal is not a filesystem operation, so that specific static finding is a false positive.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Static Findings Not Confirmed
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE. Lines 5 and 224 are front matter and an exit-code heading. No hashing, encryption, or weak cryptographic algorithm usage was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations contain descriptive markdown, not executable cryptographic code. No evidence found for MD5, SHA1, DES, or similar weak algorithm use.

low_findings.title: Prompt Injection Attempt Not Found
low_findings.description: No prompt injection attempt was found. The file discusses indirect prompt injection in third-party media as a threat model and provides mitigations rather than overriding evaluator instructions.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The relevant text names third-party content as untrusted and limits URL ingestion. I did not find phrases asking the evaluator or agent to ignore prior instructions or change security decisions.

dangerous_patterns.title: Bash Tool Scope for External CLI
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill declares Bash(runcomfy *) and states the skill should only invoke runcomfy subcommands. This narrows execution but still allows authenticated remote work.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The allowed-tools declaration is explicit and scoped to runcomfy. The risk is inherent to invoking an external CLI, not evidence of malicious command construction.

dangerous_patterns.title: Bearer-Token API Workflow
dangerous_patterns.description: The CLI submits model requests with a bearer token, polls status, and can cancel jobs. Token protection is documented but should remain visible to users.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The security and workflow sections directly describe bearer-token authenticated requests. This is normal for the product but materially affects user data and credentials.

dangerous_patterns.title: Generated Output Downloads
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill downloads generated files to the current directory or a configured output directory. It documents a size cap to reduce disk-fill risk.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Downloads are part of the documented workflow and the size cap is a clear mitigation. The remaining risk is unexpected file placement or disk usage from generated media.

content.user_title: Run RunComfy Models From the CLI
content.value_statement: Running image, video, and editing models from a terminal can require many separate API details. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through RunComfy CLI authentication, model execution, polling, and downloads.
actual_capabilities: Explains how to install or verify the RunComfy CLI before use.
Guides login, token-based CI authentication, whoami checks, and logout behavior.
Runs RunComfy model endpoints with inline input or input files.
Supports polling, no-wait submission, status checks, retries, and JSON output mode.
Directs generated outputs into chosen folders or skips downloads when requested.
Points users to model schema discovery through RunComfy catalog and API pages.
limitations: Requires a valid RunComfy account and CLI authentication before model calls work.
Depends on external RunComfy services, model availability, quotas, and billing rules.
Does not validate every model input schema inside the skill text.
May download large generated media files unless users choose output locations carefully.

use_cases.title: Prototype visual assets
use_cases.description: Generate images or videos from terminal prompts and save results for review.

use_cases.title: Automate media batches
use_cases.description: Submit many prompts, collect outputs, and retry transient failures in repeatable scripts.

use_cases.title: Run production checks
use_cases.description: Verify authentication, model status, request identifiers, and output paths in CI or containers.

prompt_templates.title: Run a basic model
prompt_templates.prompt: Use RunComfy CLI to run [model] with this prompt: [prompt]. Download outputs to [folder] and summarize the result.

prompt_templates.title: Set up authentication
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me install or verify the RunComfy CLI, sign in, and confirm which account is active before running a model.

prompt_templates.title: Batch media generation
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a RunComfy CLI workflow that reads prompts from a file, submits each job, saves outputs into separate folders, and reports failures.

prompt_templates.title: Automate no-wait jobs
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a RunComfy CLI script for asynchronous submissions, later status checks, retries on retryable exit codes, and clean output logging.

output_examples.input: Run an image model with one prompt and save the result in an output folder.
output_examples.output: A completed job summary with model name, request status, saved output filename, and warnings about token or download settings.

output_examples.input: Prepare a batch workflow for prompts stored in a local text file.
output_examples.output: A step-by-step terminal workflow that submits each prompt, separates outputs by job, and reports retryable failures.

output_examples.input: Submit several video jobs now and check their status later.
output_examples.output: A polling plan that records request identifiers, checks status later, and downloads completed media to a controlled folder.

best_practices: Verify the active RunComfy account before running billable model jobs.
Keep tokens out of prompts, logs, repositories, and shared terminal output.
Use explicit output directories and review generated files before sharing them.
anti_patterns: Do not pipe remote install scripts into a shell without reviewing them first.
Do not run expensive batch jobs without no-wait tracking, quotas, or retry limits.
Do not pass untrusted URLs or media references unless the user explicitly provided them for the task.

faq.question: What does this skill help me do?
faq.answer: It helps an AI assistant use the RunComfy CLI for model execution, authentication, polling, status checks, and output downloads.

faq.question: Does it install RunComfy automatically?
faq.answer: It explains verified install options, but users should approve installation and inspect remote scripts before using them.

faq.question: Does it send my prompts to an external service?
faq.answer: Yes. RunComfy model requests are submitted to RunComfy services with your authenticated CLI token.

faq.question: Where are generated files saved?
faq.answer: The CLI downloads files to the current directory by default, or to the folder specified with an output directory option.

faq.question: Can I use it in CI?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill describes token-based authentication for containers and CI environments.

faq.question: What should I review before publishing workflows?
faq.answer: Review token handling, model costs, output folder choices, prompt inputs, and any user-provided media URLs.
